chunk_summary: This section discusses fibrosis risk stratification in pediatric MASLD, emphasizing clinical and biochemical triggers for elastography or biopsy referral, and highlights key diagnostic tools and guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical and biochemical markers that indicate high fibrosis risk in pediatric MASLD.
Learn the role of transient elastography and its interpretation in pediatric fibrosis staging.
Identify scenarios where liver biopsy is recommended in children with suspected advanced disease.
Recognize the importance of timely referral to pediatric gastroenterology/hepatology based on risk factors.
key_facts: Persistent ALT >2× sex-specific upper limit of normal for >3 months or ALT >80 U/L are high-concern markers.
Transient elastography is recommended by 2024 ISPGHAN guidelines as a preferred noninvasive tool for fibrosis prediction.
Red flags include hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, falling platelets, AST/ALT ratio >1, or impaired synthetic function.
Biopsy is advised for children under 8 years, suspected advanced disease, or unclear diagnosis after first-line tests.
In settings without elastography, persistent biochemical elevation plus clinical red flags should prompt referral.
reference_quote: The 2024 ISPGHAN recommendations support elastography as a preferred technology for diagnosis and monitoring and treat liver stiffness measurement as a useful fibrosis predictor.
document_key_topics: Fibrosis risk stratification in pediatric MASLD
Clinical and biochemical markers for fibrosis
Role of elastography and liver biopsy in diagnosis
